name: Sonny Boy Williamson II
abstract: Alex or Aleck Miller (originally Ford, possibly December 5, 1912 – May 24, 1965), known later in his career as Sonny Boy Williamson, was an American blues harmonica player, singer and songwriter. He was an early and influential blues harp stylist who recorded successfully in the 1950s and 1960s.

about_quotes.speaker: Mose Allison
about_quotes.evidence: INTERVIEWER: I know you’d covered one of Sonny Boy Williamson’s tunes... INTERVIEWEE: No, I didn’t listen to the King Biscuit Time show. I never heard that until later. I heard about it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mose Allison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Ramsey Lewis
about_quotes.evidence: He was just a guy that worked in the shipping department. I never got his name, because they always just called him Sonny.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ramsey Lewis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Karl Berger
about_quotes.evidence: Sonny Boy Williamson was there. And he would say, “the blues is where the words are and that’s the form of the blues.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Karl Berger interviewed by Monk Rowe, Woodstock, New York, August 11, 2014

about_quotes.speaker: Branford Marsalis
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Sonny Boy Williamson? ... [INTERVIEWEE] No…Sonny Boy, too. Little Walter.
about_quotes.doc_title: Branford Marsalis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
